title: Perthes Disease
oneLiner: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head in children (4–8 years), causing limp and hip pain; early recognition preserves joint function.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to paediatric clinic by his mother with a 3-week history of a painless limp. He stopped playing football and now complains of a “tired ache” in his right knee. On examination, he walks with a Trendelenburg gait and has limited internal rotation and abduction of the right hip. There is no fever, and he is otherwise well.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Age 4–8 years** – classic window for Perthes; peak incidence at 6. Rare before 3 or after 12.
- **Male predominance** – about 4:1 male:female. Think of a boy.
- **Painless limp** – early stage often only limp without overt pain. Pain may be referred to knee (sensory overlap of obturator nerve) – classic trap.
- **Trendelenburg gait** – indicates gluteus medius weakness due to pain or mechanical insufficiency from femoral head collapse.
- **Limited internal rotation and abduction** – earliest exam finding; the joint becomes irritable. Abduction is the most sensitive motion lost in early disease.
- **No fever** – key differentiator from septic arthritis (which is hot, red, septic child).

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Bilateral Perthes** (~10%) – presents with waddling gait, mimicking neuromuscular disease or bilateral SCFE. Always check the contralateral hip on imaging.
2. **Older child (10+ years)** – atypical age; more likely to have underlying cause (e.g., hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, coagulation disorder). Poorer prognosis.
3. **Acute onset with severe pain** – rare; may be mistaken for septic arthritis or traumatic fracture. Pain out of proportion to exam should raise suspicion for pathological fracture through necrotic bone.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Perthes disease (Legg-Calvé-Perthes) is idiopathic avascular necrosis (AVN) of the capital femoral epiphysis in children. The femoral head loses its blood supply, undergoes necrosis, and then revascularises over 2–4 years. The critical clinical challenge is that the femoral head is vulnerable to deformity during the revascularisation phase.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** 1 in 1,200 children; it’s the most common cause of hip pain and limp in school-age children (after transient synovitis).
- **Stakes:** Left untreated or poorly managed, femoral head deformity leads to premature osteoarthritis by early adulthood (30s–40s).
- **Pitfall:** Knee pain referral is the #1 diagnostic trap – children with Perthes often get knee X-rays before hip X-rays, delaying diagnosis. Always examine the hip in a limping child, even if the pain is in the knee.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**
The blood supply to the femoral head in children is precarious – mainly via the lateral epiphyseal arteries (branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery). In Perthes, these arteries become occluded for reasons still unknown (no thrombus found in most cases). The epiphysis becomes ischaemic → necrosis of trabecular bone and marrow → bone resorption by osteoclasts → structural weakening → risk of fracture and collapse under weight.

**Revascularisation phase:** New blood vessels grow in from the metaphysis and synovium, bringing in osteoblasts and fibroblasts. This creates a “V” shape of granulation tissue and new bone (the “metaphyseal lucency” on X-ray). The process is uncoordinated – ossification of the necrotic template occurs but often with deformity.

**Why signs happen:**
- **Limp** – pain from synovitis and capsular irritation, plus mechanical advantage lost from femoral head collapse (Trendelenburg). The child instinctively unloads the hip.
- **Limited internal rotation** – the inflamed joint capsule tightens. Internal rotation is normally the motion with smallest range; it restricts first.
- **Knee pain** – referred via obturator nerve (hip joint innervation). The child cannot localise the hip; they point to the knee.
- **Trendelenburg sign** – gluteus medius insufficiency due to femoral head not being a stable fulcrum (or pain inhibition). The pelvis drops on the opposite side when standing on the affected leg.
- **Leg-length discrepancy** – femoral head collapse shortens the femoral neck, or capital physis may show overgrowth (coxa magna) later.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- Why age 4–8? The blood supply pattern changes around age 8–9 (Ligamentum teres artery becomes more robust). Younger children have better collateral circulation, so Perthes rarely happens after age 12.
- Why male? Androgens may affect vascular plasticity or immune response? Not fully understood, but it’s a strong epidemiological clue.
- Why no fever? It’s aseptic – no infection.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (95%) | No identifiable trigger | Classic presentation in otherwise healthy child |
| **Vascular / Thrombophilia** | Inherited thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, protein C/S deficiency) | Family history of DVT or PE; recurrent miscarriages in mother |
| **Endocrine** | Growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism, hypopituitarism | Short stature, delayed bone age, other endocrine abnormalities |
| **Coagulation** | Antiphospholipid syndrome, elevated homocysteine | Unexplained thrombosis or pregnancy loss; history of DVT in child (rare) |
| **Traumatic** | Minor repeated trauma (not single injury) – may trigger in susceptible child | Child with hypermobile joints or excessive athletic activity |
| **Bone marrow oedema** | Transient synovitis? (debated link) | Child had viral illness 1–2 weeks before limp onset |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic. Do not order thrombophilia or endocrine workup in first instance unless red flags (atypical age, family history, growth delay, bilateral disease).

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria** – No formal score, but diagnosis is clinical + radiographic. Classification systems (Catterall, Herring) are prognostic, not diagnostic.

**First-line: Pelvis AP + Frog-leg lateral X-ray**

| Finding | What It Means | Interpretation |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Widening of medial joint space | Synovitis and effusion | Early Perthes (stage I) |
| Subchondral crescentic lucency (Caffey sign) | Pathognomonic for early AVN | Evidence of ischaemic necrosis |
| Fragmentation / collapse of femoral head | Advanced disease (stage III) | Needs containment and possible surgery |
| Metaphyseal lucency (Gage sign) | Active revascularisation | Prognostic; if lateral pillar intact (Herring A/B) → good outcome |

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **Single finding that confirms diagnosis:** MRI with a T1-weighted dark band across the femoral head (infarct) or a T2-weighted high signal in the metaphysis/oedema.
- **Rule out:** Transient synovitis – normal X-ray, effusion on ultrasound, resolves in 1–2 weeks.

**Second-line: MRI with gadolinium**
- Why: Assess femoral head viability, early detection before X-ray changes, and evaluation of containment.
- Interpret: Loss of enhancement in epiphysis = active ischaemia. Revascularisation shows "double line" sign on T2.

**Ultrasound:** Evaluates effusion and can guide aspiration if septic arthritis suspected.

**Bone scan (rarely used now)** – cold defect in early stage, then increased uptake during revascularisation.

**Laboratory workup** – if atypical:
- FBC, CRP, ESR (to rule out infection)
- Coagulation panel (if bilateral, familial, or recurrence)
- Thyroid function, IGF-1 (growth hormone), bone age

**Do not:** X-ray knee in a limping child with normal knee exam – waste of time. Hip X-ray is mandatory.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning: **Goal:** Contain the necrotic femoral head in the acetabulum during the fragile revascularisation phase to prevent deformity.
sections.Immediate (first hour):
- **History and exam** – fully assess gait, range of motion, leg length. Order AP pelvis + frog-leg lateral.
- **Analgesia** – Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO/IⅤ up to 4 times daily. Avoid NSAIDs initially if suspect septic arthritis (can mask fever).
- **Non-weight-bearing** – If acute pain: crutches or wheelchair. Do not let child bear weight until pain subsides and X-ray shows no fracture.
- **Refer to paediatric orthopaedics** – all suspected Perthes need specialist review.
sections.Ward / Outpatient (24–72h):
- **Containment therapy** – the core of management. Two options:
  - **Symptomatic (non-operative):** For mild cases (age <6, Herring A/B, good range of motion). Child is immobilised in a Petrie harness (abduction orthosis) or broomstick plaster to keep hip abducted (45°), flexed (20°), and internally rotated – the “safe zone” where the femoral head is covered by acetabulum. Worn 23h/day for 6–18 months.
  - **Surgical containment:** For moderate–severe disease (age >8, Herring C, loss of abduction >15°). Varus derotation osteotomy (VDRO) or shelf acetabuloplasty. Surgery shortens the duration of containment by 6–12 months.
- **Physiotherapy** – passive range of motion exercises to prevent contracture. No forced abduction.
- **Pain control** – NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 5–10 mg/kg PO TDS) after ortho approval.
sections.Long-term:
- **Follow-up X-rays** – every 4–6 months until full revascularisation (usually 2–4 years). Monitor for femoral head deformity.
- **Osteoarthritis prevention** – if residual deformity, weight loss, sports avoidance (high-impact), possible joint preservation surgery (e.g., acetabular reorientation).
- **Total hip replacement** – if end-stage arthritis develops (typically 30s–40s). Rare in childhood.
sections.What NOT to do:
- **Do not** use immobilisation in extension/adduction – it worsens deformation.
- **Do not** force weight-bearing – collapse risk.
- **Do not** diagnose “Perthes” on a single X-ray if only medial joint widening – repeat in 6 weeks (transient synovitis self-limits).
- **Do not** start NSAIDs before ruling out septic arthritis (mask symptom of septic hip, a surgical emergency).

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Perthes (AVN) | Transient Synovitis | Septic Arthritis | Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis (SCFE) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Age** | 4–8 years | 2–10 years | Any age (peak <5) | 10–16 years (boys 13–15, girls 11–14) |
| **Pain** | Insidious limp, referred to knee | Acute/ subacute limp; pain in hip/ thigh | Acute severe pain; child will not move hip | Subacute limp with groin/ knee pain; may be stabbing |
| **Exam** | Limited IR + abd, Trendelenburg | Limited IR only (mild) | Hot, swollen, red hip; high fever, irritable child | Obligatory external rotation in flexion; leg length discrepancy |
| **X-ray** | Medial joint space widening, subchondral lucency, fragmentation | Normal or mild effusion (US only) | Soft tissue swelling, joint space widening (early) | Line of Klein's sign – epiphysis appears “slipped” |
| **Labs** | Normal ESR/CRP | Normal or mildly raised ESR | Markedly elevated CRP, ESR; PCT >0.5; WBC high | Normal ESR/CRP |
| **Key diff** | Caffey sign on X-ray | Self-limited (2 weeks) | Aspiration shows pus | Epiphysis displaced posteriorly on frog-leg view |
| **Treatment** | Containment (bracing/ surgery) | Rest + NSAIDs | Urgent surgical drainage + IV antibiotics | In situ pinning (emergency) |

**Pearl:** The child with Perthes often walks into clinic with a limp but is not systemically unwell. The child with septic arthritis will be crying, febrile, and refuse to move the leg at all.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-------|--------|
| **Femoral head deformity** (coxa plana) | Progressive limp, hip pain, leg shortening | X-ray: flattened, mushroom-shaped femoral head | Containment therapy (bracing/ surgery); if severe → femoral head reconstruction |
| **Leg-length discrepancy** | Asymmetric gait, pelvic tilt, scoliosis | Measured >2 cm difference | Shoe lift; if >2 cm consider epiphysiodesis (contralateral) |
| **Osteoarthritis** | Pain, stiffness, reduced mobility by age 30–40 | X-ray: joint space narrowing, osteophytes | NSAIDs, physiotherapy, eventually THR |
| **Acute femoral head fracture** (rare) | Acute severe pain, inability to weight-bear after fall | X-ray: subcapital fracture through necrotic bone | Emergency surgery – internal fixation or hip decompression |
| **Contracture** (flexion/adduction) | Child holds hip flexed and adducted; cannot extend fully | Passive extension limited, Thomas test positive | Physiotherapy, capsulectomy if persistent |

**Late-presentation pattern:** A 35-year-old presents with hip pain and stiffness. X-ray shows a mushroom-shaped femoral head (coxa plana) – telltale sign of untreated Perthes in childhood. The exam often finds mild Trendelenburg gait and limited rotation. The history may reveal a childhood limp parents dismissed as “growing pains.”

**What to do if untreated:** In late stage, containment is no longer beneficial. Focus on symptoms and joint preservation – activity modification, NSAIDs, physical therapy. If arthritis severe, refer for total hip replacement.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **“PERTHES”** (Key management aims):
   - **P** – Pain control (analgesia, crutches)
   - **E** – Early diagnosis (X-ray hip in limping child)
   - **R** – Range of motion exercises (prevent contracture)
   - **T** – Therapy (containment – brace or surgery)
   - **H** – Hip in safe zone (abduction + internal rotation)
   - **E** – Every 4–6 months follow-up X-ray
   - **S** – Surgery if poor prognostic factors (age >8, Herring C)

2. **“LIMP”** (Differential diagnosis of hip in child):
   - **L** – Legg-Calvé-Perthes
   - **I** – Infective (septic arthritis, TB)
   - **M** – Mechanical (SCFE, fracture)
   - **P** – Pain (transient synovitis, JIA)

3. **“TRENDELENBURG”** – In Perthes, see dropping contralateral pelvis when standing on affected leg (gluteus medius insufficiency).

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **Knee pain in a child = examine the hip.** Never X-ray the knee first in a limping child.
2. **The earliest X-ray sign might be just medial joint space widening.** Compare to the opposite side. If normal, repeat in 6 weeks.
3. **MRI is the gold standard for early diagnosis** – before X-ray changes appear. T1 dark band = infarct.
4. **Prognosis is best in children <6 years** (more vascular plasticity, less collapse). Older children (≥8) often need surgery.
5. **Bilateral Perthes (%)** – always check the contralateral hip. If bilateral, work up for endocrine or thrombophilia.
6. **The “safe zone” for containment is 45° abduction and 20° flexion.** In this position, the femoral head is deepest in the acetabulum.
7. **Do not confuse Perthes with SCFE:** SCFE occurs in obese adolescents, presents with obligatory external rotation, and is an emergency. Perthes is idiopathic and chronic.

**Common mistakes:**
- Prescribing NSAIDs and sending home a child with hip pain without X-ray – may miss septic arthritis or SCFE.
- Diagnosing Perthes on a single X-ray when actually transient synovitis – wait 2 weeks and reimage.
- Failing to refer to paediatric orthopaedics – all cases need specialist input for containment planning.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / orthopaedics on-call:**

| Situation | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Sudden severe hip pain** (cannot weight-bear) | – | Suspect pathological fracture (femoral neck fracture through necrotic bone). Urgent ortho review, X-ray, possibly CT. |
| **Fever + hip pain** (any age) | Temperature >38.5°C, CRP >50, WCC >15 | Rule out septic arthritis. Urgent ortho review, blood cultures, hip ultrasound ± aspiration. |
| **Child with limp + systemic upset** | Tachycardia, hypotension, ill appearance | Sepsis workup. Consult paediatrics. |
| **Missed diagnosis** – suspect Perthes but X-ray normal + child still limping at 2-week follow-up | – | MRI recommended. Refer to orthopaedics. |
| **Complication after management** – sudden increase in pain, fever, or deformity in child on brace | – | Urgent ortho review: possible osteomyelitis (rare), hardware failure if operated, or joint subluxation. |

**Specific thresholds for escalation (if relevant):**
- NEWS2 ≥5 in a child (modified early warning score) – escalate to senior paediatrician.
- GCS <15 in a child with new hip pain – rare, but consider fat embolism syndrome if recent trauma; call senior.
- Lactate >2 mmol/L in a child with septic arthritis – indicates severe sepsis; involve ICU.

**Key:** Perthes is rarely a medical emergency, but the mimics (septic arthritis, SCFE) are. If unsure, escalation is always safer. The rule: “A limping child with fever goes straight to orthopaedics – do not pass Go, do not give ibuprofen.”

rawMarkdown:
# Perthes Disease

**One-liner:** Avascular necrosis of the femoral head in children (4–8 years), causing limp and hip pain; early recognition preserves joint function.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

A 6-year-old boy is brought to paediatric clinic by his mother with a 3-week history of a painless limp. He stopped playing football and now complains of a “tired ache” in his right knee. On examination, he walks with a Trendelenburg gait and has limited internal rotation and abduction of the right hip. There is no fever, and he is otherwise well.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Age 4–8 years** – classic window for Perthes; peak incidence at 6. Rare before 3 or after 12.
- **Male predominance** – about 4:1 male:female. Think of a boy.
- **Painless limp** – early stage often only limp without overt pain. Pain may be referred to knee (sensory overlap of obturator nerve) – classic trap.
- **Trendelenburg gait** – indicates gluteus medius weakness due to pain or mechanical insufficiency from femoral head collapse.
- **Limited internal rotation and abduction** – earliest exam finding; the joint becomes irritable. Abduction is the most sensitive motion lost in early disease.
- **No fever** – key differentiator from septic arthritis (which is hot, red, septic child).

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Bilateral Perthes** (~10%) – presents with waddling gait, mimicking neuromuscular disease or bilateral SCFE. Always check the contralateral hip on imaging.
2. **Older child (10+ years)** – atypical age; more likely to have underlying cause (e.g., hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency, coagulation disorder). Poorer prognosis.
3. **Acute onset with severe pain** – rare; may be mistaken for septic arthritis or traumatic fracture. Pain out of proportion to exam should raise suspicion for pathological fracture through necrotic bone.

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Perthes disease (Legg-Calvé-Perthes) is idiopathic avascular necrosis (AVN) of the capital femoral epiphysis in children. The femoral head loses its blood supply, undergoes necrosis, and then revascularises over 2–4 years. The critical clinical challenge is that the femoral head is vulnerable to deformity during the revascularisation phase.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** 1 in 1,200 children; it’s the most common cause of hip pain and limp in school-age children (after transient synovitis).
- **Stakes:** Left untreated or poorly managed, femoral head deformity leads to premature osteoarthritis by early adulthood (30s–40s).
- **Pitfall:** Knee pain referral is the #1 diagnostic trap – children with Perthes often get knee X-rays before hip X-rays, delaying diagnosis. Always examine the hip in a limping child, even if the pain is in the knee.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**
The blood supply to the femoral head in children is precarious – mainly via the lateral epiphyseal arteries (branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery). In Perthes, these arteries become occluded for reasons still unknown (no thrombus found in most cases). The epiphysis becomes ischaemic → necrosis of trabecular bone and marrow → bone resorption by osteoclasts → structural weakening → risk of fracture and collapse under weight.

**Revascularisation phase:** New blood vessels grow in from the metaphysis and synovium, bringing in osteoblasts and fibroblasts. This creates a “V” shape of granulation tissue and new bone (the “metaphyseal lucency” on X-ray). The process is uncoordinated – ossification of the necrotic template occurs but often with deformity.

**Why signs happen:**
- **Limp** – pain from synovitis and capsular irritation, plus mechanical advantage lost from femoral head collapse (Trendelenburg). The child instinctively unloads the hip.
- **Limited internal rotation** – the inflamed joint capsule tightens. Internal rotation is normally the motion with smallest range; it restricts first.
- **Knee pain** – referred via obturator nerve (hip joint innervation). The child cannot localise the hip; they point to the knee.
- **Trendelenburg sign** – gluteus medius insufficiency due to femoral head not being a stable fulcrum (or pain inhibition). The pelvis drops on the opposite side when standing on the affected leg.
- **Leg-length discrepancy** – femoral head collapse shortens the femoral neck, or capital physis may show overgrowth (coxa magna) later.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- Why age 4–8? The blood supply pattern changes around age 8–9 (Ligamentum teres artery becomes more robust). Younger children have better collateral circulation, so Perthes rarely happens after age 12.
- Why male? Androgens may affect vascular plasticity or immune response? Not fully understood, but it’s a strong epidemiological clue.
- Why no fever? It’s aseptic – no infection.

---

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (95%) | No identifiable trigger | Classic presentation in otherwise healthy child |
| **Vascular / Thrombophilia** | Inherited thrombophilia (Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, protein C/S deficiency) | Family history of DVT or PE; recurrent miscarriages in mother |
| **Endocrine** | Growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism, hypopituitarism | Short stature, delayed bone age, other endocrine abnormalities |
| **Coagulation** | Antiphospholipid syndrome, elevated homocysteine | Unexplained thrombosis or pregnancy loss; history of DVT in child (rare) |
| **Traumatic** | Minor repeated trauma (not single injury) – may trigger in susceptible child | Child with hypermobile joints or excessive athletic activity |
| **Bone marrow oedema** | Transient synovitis? (debated link) | Child had viral illness 1–2 weeks before limp onset |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic. Do not order thrombophilia or endocrine workup in first instance unless red flags (atypical age, family history, growth delay, bilateral disease).

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria** – No formal score, but diagnosis is clinical + radiographic. Classification systems (Catterall, Herring) are prognostic, not diagnostic.

**First-line: Pelvis AP + Frog-leg lateral X-ray**

| Finding | What It Means | Interpretation |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Widening of medial joint space | Synovitis and effusion | Early Perthes (stage I) |
| Subchondral crescentic lucency (Caffey sign) | Pathognomonic for early AVN | Evidence of ischaemic necrosis |
| Fragmentation / collapse of femoral head | Advanced disease (stage III) | Needs containment and possible surgery |
| Metaphyseal lucency (Gage sign) | Active revascularisation | Prognostic; if lateral pillar intact (Herring A/B) → good outcome |

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **Single finding that confirms diagnosis:** MRI with a T1-weighted dark band across the femoral head (infarct) or a T2-weighted high signal in the metaphysis/oedema.
- **Rule out:** Transient synovitis – normal X-ray, effusion on ultrasound, resolves in 1–2 weeks.

**Second-line: MRI with gadolinium**
- Why: Assess femoral head viability, early detection before X-ray changes, and evaluation of containment.
- Interpret: Loss of enhancement in epiphysis = active ischaemia. Revascularisation shows "double line" sign on T2.

**Ultrasound:** Evaluates effusion and can guide aspiration if septic arthritis suspected.

**Bone scan (rarely used now)** – cold defect in early stage, then increased uptake during revascularisation.

**Laboratory workup** – if atypical:
- FBC, CRP, ESR (to rule out infection)
- Coagulation panel (if bilateral, familial, or recurrence)
- Thyroid function, IGF-1 (growth hormone), bone age

**Do not:** X-ray knee in a limping child with normal knee exam – waste of time. Hip X-ray is mandatory.

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Goal:** Contain the necrotic femoral head in the acetabulum during the fragile revascularisation phase to prevent deformity.

### Immediate (first hour)
- **History and exam** – fully assess gait, range of motion, leg length. Order AP pelvis + frog-leg lateral.
- **Analgesia** – Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO/IⅤ up to 4 times daily. Avoid NSAIDs initially if suspect septic arthritis (can mask fever).
- **Non-weight-bearing** – If acute pain: crutches or wheelchair. Do not let child bear weight until pain subsides and X-ray shows no fracture.
- **Refer to paediatric orthopaedics** – all suspected Perthes need specialist review.

### Ward / Outpatient (24–72h)
- **Containment therapy** – the core of management. Two options:
  - **Symptomatic (non-operative):** For mild cases (age <6, Herring A/B, good range of motion). Child is immobilised in a Petrie harness (abduction orthosis) or broomstick plaster to keep hip abducted (45°), flexed (20°), and internally rotated – the “safe zone” where the femoral head is covered by acetabulum. Worn 23h/day for 6–18 months.
  - **Surgical containment:** For moderate–severe disease (age >8, Herring C, loss of abduction >15°). Varus derotation osteotomy (VDRO) or shelf acetabuloplasty. Surgery shortens the duration of containment by 6–12 months.
- **Physiotherapy** – passive range of motion exercises to prevent contracture. No forced abduction.
- **Pain control** – NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 5–10 mg/kg PO TDS) after ortho approval.

### Long-term
- **Follow-up X-rays** – every 4–6 months until full revascularisation (usually 2–4 years). Monitor for femoral head deformity.
- **Osteoarthritis prevention** – if residual deformity, weight loss, sports avoidance (high-impact), possible joint preservation surgery (e.g., acetabular reorientation).
- **Total hip replacement** – if end-stage arthritis develops (typically 30s–40s). Rare in childhood.

### What NOT to do
- **Do not** use immobilisation in extension/adduction – it worsens deformation.
- **Do not** force weight-bearing – collapse risk.
- **Do not** diagnose “Perthes” on a single X-ray if only medial joint widening – repeat in 6 weeks (transient synovitis self-limits).
- **Do not** start NSAIDs before ruling out septic arthritis (mask symptom of septic hip, a surgical emergency).

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Perthes (AVN) | Transient Synovitis | Septic Arthritis | Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis (SCFE) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Age** | 4–8 years | 2–10 years | Any age (peak <5) | 10–16 years (boys 13–15, girls 11–14) |
| **Pain** | Insidious limp, referred to knee | Acute/ subacute limp; pain in hip/ thigh | Acute severe pain; child will not move hip | Subacute limp with groin/ knee pain; may be stabbing |
| **Exam** | Limited IR + abd, Trendelenburg | Limited IR only (mild) | Hot, swollen, red hip; high fever, irritable child | Obligatory external rotation in flexion; leg length discrepancy |
| **X-ray** | Medial joint space widening, subchondral lucency, fragmentation | Normal or mild effusion (US only) | Soft tissue swelling, joint space widening (early) | Line of Klein's sign – epiphysis appears “slipped” |
| **Labs** | Normal ESR/CRP | Normal or mildly raised ESR | Markedly elevated CRP, ESR; PCT >0.5; WBC high | Normal ESR/CRP |
| **Key diff** | Caffey sign on X-ray | Self-limited (2 weeks) | Aspiration shows pus | Epiphysis displaced posteriorly on frog-leg view |
| **Treatment** | Containment (bracing/ surgery) | Rest + NSAIDs | Urgent surgical drainage + IV antibiotics | In situ pinning (emergency) |

**Pearl:** The child with Perthes often walks into clinic with a limp but is not systemically unwell. The child with septic arthritis will be crying, febrile, and refuse to move the leg at all.

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-------|--------|
| **Femoral head deformity** (coxa plana) | Progressive limp, hip pain, leg shortening | X-ray: flattened, mushroom-shaped femoral head | Containment therapy (bracing/ surgery); if severe → femoral head reconstruction |
| **Leg-length discrepancy** | Asymmetric gait, pelvic tilt, scoliosis | Measured >2 cm difference | Shoe lift; if >2 cm consider epiphysiodesis (contralateral) |
| **Osteoarthritis** | Pain, stiffness, reduced mobility by age 30–40 | X-ray: joint space narrowing, osteophytes | NSAIDs, physiotherapy, eventually THR |
| **Acute femoral head fracture** (rare) | Acute severe pain, inability to weight-bear after fall | X-ray: subcapital fracture through necrotic bone | Emergency surgery – internal fixation or hip decompression |
| **Contracture** (flexion/adduction) | Child holds hip flexed and adducted; cannot extend fully | Passive extension limited, Thomas test positive | Physiotherapy, capsulectomy if persistent |

**Late-presentation pattern:** A 35-year-old presents with hip pain and stiffness. X-ray shows a mushroom-shaped femoral head (coxa plana) – telltale sign of untreated Perthes in childhood. The exam often finds mild Trendelenburg gait and limited rotation. The history may reveal a childhood limp parents dismissed as “growing pains.”

**What to do if untreated:** In late stage, containment is no longer beneficial. Focus on symptoms and joint preservation – activity modification, NSAIDs, physical therapy. If arthritis severe, refer for total hip replacement.

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **“PERTHES”** (Key management aims):
   - **P** – Pain control (analgesia, crutches)
   - **E** – Early diagnosis (X-ray hip in limping child)
   - **R** – Range of motion exercises (prevent contracture)
   - **T** – Therapy (containment – brace or surgery)
   - **H** – Hip in safe zone (abduction + internal rotation)
   - **E** – Every 4–6 months follow-up X-ray
   - **S** – Surgery if poor prognostic factors (age >8, Herring C)

2. **“LIMP”** (Differential diagnosis of hip in child):
   - **L** – Legg-Calvé-Perthes
   - **I** – Infective (septic arthritis, TB)
   - **M** – Mechanical (SCFE, fracture)
   - **P** – Pain (transient synovitis, JIA)

3. **“TRENDELENBURG”** – In Perthes, see dropping contralateral pelvis when standing on affected leg (gluteus medius insufficiency).

**Clinical pearls:**

1. **Knee pain in a child = examine the hip.** Never X-ray the knee first in a limping child.
2. **The earliest X-ray sign might be just medial joint space widening.** Compare to the opposite side. If normal, repeat in 6 weeks.
3. **MRI is the gold standard for early diagnosis** – before X-ray changes appear. T1 dark band = infarct.
4. **Prognosis is best in children <6 years** (more vascular plasticity, less collapse). Older children (≥8) often need surgery.
5. **Bilateral Perthes (%)** – always check the contralateral hip. If bilateral, work up for endocrine or thrombophilia.
6. **The “safe zone” for containment is 45° abduction and 20° flexion.** In this position, the femoral head is deepest in the acetabulum.
7. **Do not confuse Perthes with SCFE:** SCFE occurs in obese adolescents, presents with obligatory external rotation, and is an emergency. Perthes is idiopathic and chronic.

**Common mistakes:**
- Prescribing NSAIDs and sending home a child with hip pain without X-ray – may miss septic arthritis or SCFE.
- Diagnosing Perthes on a single X-ray when actually transient synovitis – wait 2 weeks and reimage.
- Failing to refer to paediatric orthopaedics – all cases need specialist input for containment planning.

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / orthopaedics on-call:**

| Situation | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **Sudden severe hip pain** (cannot weight-bear) | – | Suspect pathological fracture (femoral neck fracture through necrotic bone). Urgent ortho review, X-ray, possibly CT. |
| **Fever + hip pain** (any age) | Temperature >38.5°C, CRP >50, WCC >15 | Rule out septic arthritis. Urgent ortho review, blood cultures, hip ultrasound ± aspiration. |
| **Child with limp + systemic upset** | Tachycardia, hypotension, ill appearance | Sepsis workup. Consult paediatrics. |
| **Missed diagnosis** – suspect Perthes but X-ray normal + child still limping at 2-week follow-up | – | MRI recommended. Refer to orthopaedics. |
| **Complication after management** – sudden increase in pain, fever, or deformity in child on brace | – | Urgent ortho review: possible osteomyelitis (rare), hardware failure if operated, or joint subluxation. |

**Specific thresholds for escalation (if relevant):**
- NEWS2 ≥5 in a child (modified early warning score) – escalate to senior paediatrician.
- GCS <15 in a child with new hip pain – rare, but consider fat embolism syndrome if recent trauma; call senior.
- Lactate >2 mmol/L in a child with septic arthritis – indicates severe sepsis; involve ICU.

**Key:** Perthes is rarely a medical emergency, but the mimics (septic arthritis, SCFE) are. If unsure, escalation is always safer. The rule: “A limping child with fever goes straight to orthopaedics – do not pass Go, do not give ibuprofen.”
